text: Overtraining Syndrome (OTS) is individualized and sport-specific in its signs and symptoms, meaning no single universal diagnostic marker or symptom profile reliably identifies it across all athletes.
No definitive diagnostic biomarker for Overtraining Syndrome has been established despite over 90 years of research, requiring coaches to rely on multi-domain monitoring rather than any single test.

OTS results from non-linear interactions of multiple predisposing factors including hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis dysfunction, intestinal microbiota disruption, immune dysfunction, and low energy availability, not from training load alone.
Low energy availability is a predisposing factor for Overtraining Syndrome, meaning adequate caloric intake relative to training load is important for OTS prevention.

Effective OTS prevention and management requires concurrent monitoring across multiple domains (neural, hormonal, gut, immune, and energy availability) rather than tracking any single variable in isolation.
